subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7! In this unit you will discover three different ways of describing the very same thing: a part of a whole. Those three ways are fractions, decimals and percentages. They look different on the page, but they are three languages telling the same story. Learning to move smoothly between them is one of the most useful skills in all of mathematics, and you will use it for the rest of your life.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself on paper before you look at the answer, and only move on when the idea feels clear. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a different way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Because parts of a whole are the language of fairness, of trade and of measurement. Traders use percentages to work out discounts and profit. Cooks use fractions to scale a recipe up for a big Eid meal. Families use decimals when they measure water or count shillings and cents. Even sharing charity, or sadaqah, fairly among people uses the idea of splitting a whole into equal parts. When you become confident with fractions, decimals and percentages, you gain a tool you will reach for almost every day.

As you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the bars, shade the parts, write the conversions and say the equivalents out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
Name the numerator and denominator of a fraction and explain what each one tells you.
Find a fraction of an amount using the divide-then-multiply method.
Recognise proper fractions, improper fractions and mixed numbers, and convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers.
Explain that a percentage means ‘out of 100’ and that a decimal shows tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
Convert between fractions, decimals and percentages in both directions.
Recognise and recall the common equivalents (such as 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%).
Compare and order a mixed set of fractions, decimals and percentages by converting them to the same form.

concepts.title: Understanding Fractions
concepts.explanation: A fraction shows a part of a whole. Picture a round anjero cut into equal pieces to share fairly. If you cut it into 4 equal pieces and take 3 of them, you have taken 3/4 of the anjero. The whole was split into 4 equal parts, and you have 3 of those parts. Every fraction has two numbers, one above the other, and each has its own job:
concepts.example: The numerator is the top number. It tells you how many parts you have.

concepts.title: Finding a Fraction of an Amount
concepts.explanation: One of the most useful things you can do with a fraction is to find a fraction of a quantity – for example, 3/4 of 20 sweets, or 2/5 of 30 shillings. There is a simple two-step method that always works: Divide the amount by the denominator (the bottom number).
concepts.example: Multiply the answer by the numerator (the top number).

concepts.title: Types of Fractions
concepts.explanation: Not all fractions are smaller than one whole. There are three important kinds of fraction, and you should be able to recognise each one at a glance. Proper fractions have a numerator that is smaller than the denominator. Because you have fewer parts than a whole set, a proper fraction is always less than 1. Examples are 1/2, 3/4 and 5/8. If you have 5/8 of a chocolate bar, you have less than a whole bar.
concepts.example: Improper fractions have a numerator that is larger than the denominator. Because you have more parts than one whole set, an improper fraction is always more than 1. Examples are 5/4, 7/3 and 9/2. If you have 5/4 of a…

concepts.title: Decimals and Percentages
concepts.explanation: A decimal uses a decimal point to show parts of a whole that are smaller than one. The digits after the point stand for tenths, hundredths and thousandths, getting ten times smaller each step to the right. 0.5 means 5 tenths – the same as 5/10, which is one half.
concepts.example: 0.25 means 25 hundredths – the same as 25/100, which is one quarter.

concepts.title: Converting Between the Three Forms
concepts.explanation: This is the heart of the unit. Fractions, decimals and percentages are three ways of writing the same amount, so you must be able to travel between them in any direction. Here are the reliable methods. Method 1 – make the denominator 100. Because a percentage is a number out of 100, change the fraction into an equal fraction with 100 on the bottom, then the numerator is the percentage. For 1/4: multiply top and bottom by 25 to get 25/100, so 1/4 = 25%. For 3/5: multiply top and bottom by 20 to get 60/100, so 3/5 =…
concepts.example: Method 2 – turn it into a decimal first, then multiply by 100. For 1/4, divide 1 by 4 to get 0.25, then 0.25 × 100 = 25%. Both methods give the same answer, so use whichever feels easier for the numbers in front of you.

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering
concepts.explanation: Sometimes you are given a mixture of fractions, decimals and percentages and asked which is largest, or to put them in order. You cannot compare them fairly while they wear different clothes, so the golden rule is: convert them all to the same form first, then compare. Percentages or decimals are usually the easiest form to compare, because they are simply hundredths or tenths. Let us order 0.6, 55% and 3/5 from smallest to largest. Convert everything to percentages: 0.6 = 60%, 55% stays as 55%, and 3/5 = 60%. Now…
concepts.example: Here is a second example. Order 0.7, 65%, 3/4 and 0.8. As decimals these are 0.7, 0.65, 0.75 and 0.8. Lined up smallest to largest: 0.65, 0.7, 0.75, 0.8. So the original order is 65%, 0.7, 3/4, 0.8. Converting to a…

methods.title: How to find a fraction of an amount
methods.example: In the fraction 4/7, what is the numerator, what is the denominator, and what does the fraction mean?
steps: Divide the amount by the denominator (this gives one part).
Multiply that answer by the numerator.
Write the result with any units, such as shillings or cups.

methods.title: How to convert an improper fraction to a mixed number
steps: Divide the numerator by the denominator.
The whole-number answer is the whole part.
The remainder becomes the new numerator over the same denominator.

methods.title: How to find a percentage of an amount
steps: Change the percentage to a decimal or an easy fraction.
Multiply it by the amount (or use 10% and 1% building blocks).
For a discount, subtract the saving from the original price.

methods.title: Convert a percentage to a decimal
steps: Solution: Divide by 100, moving the point two places: 60% = 60/100 = 0.6.
Check the convert a percentage to a decimal result carefully.
Check the convert a percentage to a decimal result carefully.

methods.title: Improper fraction to mixed number
steps: Solution: Divide the numerator by the denominator: 11 ÷ 4 = 2 remainder 3.
The whole part is 2 and the remainder 3 goes over 4, so 11/4 = 2 3/4.

methods.title: Order a mixed set
steps: Solution: Convert all to percentages: 1/2 = 50%, 45% = 45%, 0.6 = 60%.
In order: 45%, 50%, 60%, so the answer is 45%, 1/2, 0.6.
Check the order a mixed set result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Name the parts of a fraction
workedExamples.prompt: In the fraction 4/7, what is the numerator, what is the denominator, and what does the fraction mean?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The numerator is 4 (the top number) and the denominator is 7 (the bottom number). The fraction 4/7 means 4 parts out of 7 equal parts of a whole.

workedExamples.title: Find a fraction of an amount
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide by the denominator: 30 ÷ 5 = 6 (one fifth is 6). Multiply by the numerator: 6 × 2 = 12. So 2/5 of 30 = 12.

workedExamples.title: Convert a fraction to a percentage
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Make the denominator 100. Multiply top and bottom by 25: 3/4 = 75/100 = 75%. (Check with decimals: 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75 = 75%.)

workedExamples.title: Convert a percentage to a decimal
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide by 100, moving the point two places: 60% = 60/100 = 0.6.

workedExamples.title: Improper fraction to mixed number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide the numerator by the denominator: 11 ÷ 4 = 2 remainder 3. The whole part is 2 and the remainder 3 goes over 4, so 11/4 = 2 3/4. Check: 2 × 4 + 3 = 11. ✓

workedExamples.title: Order a mixed set
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Convert all to percentages: 1/2 = 50%, 45% = 45%, 0.6 = 60%. In order: 45%, 50%, 60%, so the answer is 45%, 1/2, 0.6.

workedExamples.title: Find a percentage of an amount (discount)
workedExamples.prompt: A jacket costs 80 shillings. It is on sale for 25% off. What is the sale price?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find 25% of 80: that is a quarter, 80 ÷ 4 = 20 shillings saved. Subtract from the price: 80 − 20 = 60 shillings.

workedExamples.title: Score as a percentage
workedExamples.prompt: A basketball player makes 18 out of 24 free throws. Write this as a fraction and as a percentage.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: As a fraction it is 18/24, which simplifies to 3/4 (divide top and bottom by 6). And 3/4 = 75%, so the success rate is 75%.

workedExamples.title: Convert to mixed numbers
workedExamples.prompt: Convert to mixed numbers: a) 9/4 b) 11/3 c) 15/8.

workedExamples.title: Convert to improper fractions
workedExamples.prompt: Convert to improper fractions: a) 2 1/3 b) 1 3/4 c) 3 2/5.

workedExamples.title: Circle the largest value: 0.6, 55%, 3/5, 0.58
workedExamples.solution: Largest is 3/5 (= 0.6 = 60%), tying with 0.6; both beat 55% and 0.58.

activities.title: Fraction Anjero Party
steps: Cut out six paper circles – these are your ‘anjero’.
Cut circle 1 into 2 equal pieces and colour 1 piece (1/2). Cut circle 2 into 4 equal pieces and colour 3 (3/4).
Cut circle 3 into 8 equal pieces and colour 5 (5/8). Cut circle 4 into 5 equal pieces and colour 2 (2/5).
For each anjero, write the fraction coloured as a fraction, a decimal and a percentage.
Challenge: Which anjero has the most left over? Which two show equivalent fractions?

activities.title: Percentage Detective
activities.materials: newspapers, shop flyers, or food labels.
steps: Go on a ‘percentage hunt’ and find ten different uses of percentages.
Write each one down and explain in words what it means (for example, ‘30% off’ means you save 30 shillings in every 100).
Choose five of your percentages and convert each one to a fraction and a decimal.
Share your favourite discovery with a family member.

activities.title: Equivalents Memory Game
steps: Make 12 cards: four fractions (1/2, 1/4, 3/4, 1/5), four decimals (0.5, 0.25, 0.75, 0.2), four percentages (50%, 25%, 75%, 20%).
Shuffle all the cards and lay them face down in a grid.
Take turns flipping three cards. If all three show the same value (such as 1/4, 0.25 and 25%), keep the set.
The player with the most matching sets wins.

activities.title: Shopping Smart Investigation
activities.materials: shop flyers or pretend price tags, paper and pencil.
steps: Choose five items and give each a price in shillings and a percentage discount.
For each item work out the discount amount and the final sale price.
You have 100 shillings to spend – which sale items give you the best value?
Write a short sentence explaining how you decided.
This investigation turns percentages into real money decisions, just like a trader in the market.

activities.title: Number Line Washing Line (Family Game)
activities.materials: string, paper cards, pegs or tape, your family.
steps: Stretch a string across the room – the left end is 0 and the right end is 1.
Write cards such as 1/2, 0.25, 75%, 3/5 and 0.9.
Take turns pegging each card in the right place along the line.
Discuss any cards that belong at the same spot, such as 1/2 and 50%.

activities.title: Decimal Place-Value City
activities.materials: large paper, colouring pencils, small objects such as beans or bottle caps.
steps: Draw a city with three streets: Ones Avenue, Tenths Street and Hundredths Boulevard.
Place objects on the streets to build a decimal, for example 2 on Ones and 3 on Tenths makes 2.3.
Build five different decimals and write each one down.
Convert each decimal to a fraction and, where you can, a percentage.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand how fractions, decimals and percentages connect? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Fraction of an Amount Divide by the denominator (bottom), then multiply by the numerator (top). 3/4 of 20: 20 divided by 4 is 5, then 5 times 3 is 15.

rules.title: Converting Rule
rules.text: Fraction to percentage: make the bottom 100, or divide top by bottom then times 100. Percentage to decimal: divide by 100. Decimal to percentage: multiply by 100. Percentage to fraction: write over 100 and simplify.

rules.title: Comparing Rule
rules.text: To compare or order, change everything to the same form first. Percentages or decimals are usually the easiest form to line up.

terms: A number showing part of a whole
The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
A number using a point to show tenths, hundredths, thousandths
A number out of 100, shown with %
Different forms that mean the same amount
A whole number plus a fraction, like 2 1/3
commonMistakes: Only add fractions with the same denominator
A percentage is always out of 100
2 1/4 means two wholes and a quarter; 21/4 is an improper fraction

realProblems.prompt: A jacket costs 80 shillings. It is on sale for 30% off. What is the sale price?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Sahra runs 1.5 km on Monday and 1 3/4 km on Tuesday. On which day did she run farther, and how do you know?
realProblems.answer: Tuesday – 1 3/4 km = 1.75 km, which is more than 1.5 km.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Tuesday – 1 3/4 km = 1.75 km, which is more than 1.5 km.

realProblems.prompt: Tom saves 1/5 of his 15-shilling allowance each week. How much does he save, expressed both as shillings and as a percentage?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A school in Kismaayo has 200 students. If 3/4 of them play sport, how many students play sport, and what percentage is that?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A recipe calls for 2 1/4 cups of flour. You have 2.2 cups. Do you have enough? Explain your answer.
realProblems.answer: No – 2 1/4 = 2.25 cups, and 2.25 > 2.2, so you are a little short.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: No – 2 1/4 = 2.25 cups, and 2.25 > 2.2, so you are a little short.

realProblems.prompt: In an art class, 0.6 of the students chose painting and 25% chose sculpture. The rest chose drawing. What fraction chose drawing?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find the odd one out and explain why: 1/5, 0.2, 25%, 20%.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 25% is the odd one – it equals 0.25 while the other three all equal 0.2.

questions.question: What does “Fraction” mean?
options: A number showing part of a whole
The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
A number using a point to show tenths, hundredths, thousandths
questions.answer: A number showing part of a whole
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Fraction means A number showing part of a whole.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself on paper before you look at the answer, and only move on when the idea feels clear. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a different way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that still feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Because parts of a whole are the language of fairness, of trade and of measurement. Traders use percentages to work out discounts and profit. Cooks use fractions to scale a recipe up for a big Eid meal. Families use decimals when they measure water or count shillings and cents. Even sharing charity, or sadaqah, fairly among people uses the idea of splitting a whole into equal parts. When you become confident with fractions, decimals and percentages, you gain a tool you will reach for almost every day.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the bars, shade the parts, write the conversions and say the equivalents out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
I can name the numerator and denominator of a fraction and explain what each one tells you.
I can find a fraction of an amount using the divide-then-multiply method.
I can recognise proper fractions, improper fractions and mixed numbers, and convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers.
I can explain that a percentage means ‘out of 100’ and that a decimal shows tenths, hundredths and thousandths.
I can convert between fractions, decimals and percentages in both directions.
